notes: Core subject of study; enterprise adoption of ATA disk as complementary/midline tier. 7% of respondents used 80%+ ATA; 50% used none at all in 2003.
Maxtor's enterprise-targeted ATA disk; designed for 24/7 operation with higher MTBF than desktop ATA. Positioned as 'complementary' midline storage. Acquired by Seagate 2006.
Evolution path from parallel ATA; became dominant nearline enterprise interface 2004-2012. Predicted by study as next step.
Dominant high-performance enterprise storage interface; 23.6% of respondents had 60%+ FC. Premium performance tier.
50.8% of respondents had 60%+ SCSI storage. Still dominant in 2003 but declining vs FC.
Progress in networked storage noted; but DAS still prevalent. Study recommends ATA solutions must work in DAS as well as SAN/NAS.
Growing; but slower than vendors hoped. Study recommends ATA solutions must work in NAS environments.
Still a major storage topology in 2003; DAS will continue to be significant per study finding.
Study recommends MTBF model on 1TB mirrored configuration to show MaXLine advantage over plain ATA.
Dominant backup medium; backup/restore pain point persists. Disk-based backup opportunity identified as major growth vector.
Read-only/seldom-written candidate for midline storage; but managers cannot easily identify percentage.
Read-write workload requiring high-performance tier; not a midline candidate. Bipolar response on read-only percentage.
Study identifies as major opportunity for midline/ATA disk. Backup-to-disk market grew dramatically 2004-2010. Opportunity driven by backup window pain.
Study concludes ISV partnership with storage management/ILM software is required to realize tiered storage potential. 'Policy management for storage migration.' Proved correct.
Study coins/validates 'mid-line' as the correct market term. Now standard nomenclature for capacity-optimized enterprise disk (nearline SAS/SATA).
Over half of respondents had mainframe storage >33% of total. 'Mainframe is not dead' conclusion.
82% had Windows storage >33% of total; 35% had >70%. Windows benefiting at expense of Unix/Linux.
